claim: Biological individuation in living organisms can be operationalized as the continuation of active maintenance across immune, neural, and microbiome subsystems; individuation terminates when integrated maintenance fails.
statement: Biological individuation in living organisms can be operationalized as the continuation of active maintenance across immune, neural, and microbiome subsystems; individuation terminates when integrated maintenance fails.
scope: Living biological organisms under active metabolism; explicitly excludes legal or social identity, non-living systems, and identity persistence through discontinuity (coma, anesthesia, transplant)
assumptions: Active metabolic maintenance (autopoiesis) is measurable across immune, neural, and microbiome subsystems.
Integrated maintenance is quantifiable as cross-subsystem coupling under energetic throughput.
Biological individuation is strictly distinct from legal, social, or philosophical identity.
formal_objects: Autopoietic closure A: set of processes maintaining the system's own production
Integration metric I(subsystems): cross-subsystem coupling measure under metabolic constraint
Maintenance capacity M: energy flux sustaining homeostatic processes
differentiators: Removed 'the self is what maintains coherence' as an identity definition: coma patients retain social/legal identity with minimal autonomous maintenance; claim was too strong as a definition of self.
Removed philosophical scope: claim now operationalizes BIOLOGICAL LIFE, not selfhood or personal identity.
Does not individuate identical twins: same maintenance architecture does not produce distinct individuation metrics — twin individuation is positional/geometric, not subsystem-compositional.

break_tests.setup: Person in medically induced coma, full external life support, no autonomous integration.
break_tests.expected: If the self = coherence maintenance, coma patient has no self during coma.
break_tests.observed: Legal, medical, and social identity are preserved throughout. Individual is recognized as the same person.
break_tests.verdict: FAIL — identity-as-maintenance definition. PASS — biological individuation claim (external support substitutes M; the individuation criterion applies to the system including support).

break_tests.setup: Identical twins at birth. Same DNA, overlapping microbiome, compatible immune systems.
break_tests.expected: If subsystem composition defines individuation, twins should not be individuable by this metric at birth.
break_tests.observed: Twins are spatially and numerically distinct organisms. Individuation is positional, not compositional. The metric fails to distinguish them.
break_tests.verdict: FAIL — individuation by subsystem composition. PASS — biological maintenance is confirmed operational in both; the failure is in individuation resolution, not maintenance operationalization.

break_tests.setup: Brain-dead patient with functioning heart, lungs, gut maintained by ventilator. Some subsystems active.
break_tests.expected: If maintenance = individuation, partial maintenance should yield partial individuation.
break_tests.observed: Medical definition of death at brain-death despite remaining subsystem activity. Shows that which subsystems are definitionally required must be specified.
break_tests.verdict: PARTIAL FAIL — claim requires specification of which subsystems are necessary vs. sufficient for the M criterion.

falsifiers: A living organism (by any standard metric) maintaining individuation with zero active maintenance across immune, neural, and microbiome systems.
A formally dead organism (by all standard metrics) sustaining full active integration across all three specified subsystems.
